item.title: Re: Interesting
item.content:encoded:
I don't hate it. I hate its popularity because its a major example of my early sophomoric writing here.

Think of George Lucas and the Star Wars revision/rerelease fuckery. Part of me desperately wants to do that to this (but without the uproar). Part of me wants to preserve this as part of the history of my development as a writer. All of me can't read it without cringing.

item.title: Interesting
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted, possibly because my new favorite thing about this skip is that the author hates it intensely.

Honestly, if the linked pages were fixed and a handful of things were written, this could be a very very good SCP. I like the implications.

item.title: Re: Glitch or intentional?
item.content:encoded: Intentional.

item.title: Glitch or intentional?
item.content:encoded: The main file for 2237 keeps switching between the "Alpha" and "Bravo" versions for me: as in, if I open it, it can be either one of them. Is this a glitch or intentionally designed like this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this! It makes sense that the Foundation would have multiple failsafes. Don’t be so self-critical Agent; this is much better than anything I could write!

+1

item.title: Re: Substitute Spaceships
item.content:encoded: I would not be opposed to this; however, my primary objection rises from the fact that 2722 was actually good.

item.title: Substitute Spaceships
item.content:encoded: I think it might be feasible in some cases to adjust prior links to the old SCP-2722 to use this instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: D'aww… Now I'm sad to the point of being inconsolable

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations! You found one of my worst SCPs! We're sorry but we're all out of consolation prizes at the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Either I'm missing something or both Alfa and Beta link back to themselves? Seems redundant and confusing…
wtf

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well if you include that theory, it might survive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this at +90? Ew. Stop it.

item.title: Interesting concept I'd change one thing though
item.content:encoded:
Change the date in the document from the future to a few years ago and have it be the stored message to be shown to the crew instead of a message that was read by a future crew.

It doesn't make sense we're reading it now, but I do like that it implies humans came to earth on this thing and the reality bending technology "seeded" us here and it would make sense for the foundation to keep this thing operational as a fail safe if all other options for human survival fall through for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for the late reply but the problem with that theory is that Kalinin's 001 is actually good while this is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possible headcanon: every time a Serapis event (Kalinin's 001) happens, the Foundation of that world launches one of these. At some point in the cycle, interaction with a Space-Time anomaly creates the human civilization in the Hydra supercluster, thus necessitating the cycle in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or the part where these ships just casually rewrite entire planets? Like, entirely?

Edit: Also, what the hell is wrong with me? I gotta rewrite this already…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did you read both iterations? This Earth is a copy of the original (and may be a copy of a copy of a copy…).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i don't get it why is this an SCP? what's? anomalous about it? they're just spaceships. high-tech sure but they were created by the Foundation using well understood technology. if they would be created by aliens or whatever then it would be an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Derp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still see "Her Majesty's Foundation" in the paragraph about the city library…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's supposed to be "His," because the UK had a King at the time. Fixed now.

I haven't looked at this or worked on the rewrite in a long time. I need to get on top of that…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it supposed to be His Majesty's Foundation or Her Majesty's Foundation? Both are used within Document Bravo.

I think my favorite part about this article is the shift in perspective between the two documents, and the resulting puzzle of "how can this abandoned sci-fi city in the desert a hundred years ago be the same thing as this fleet of spaceships orbiting the Sun today" until it clicks. I would be willing to upvote for that (and the fact that the Bravo version looks like that other SCP that's a buried city from the future and then really isn't).

Looking at the comments, though, clearly there was a lot more than that going into the article's creation, so I wouldn't mind seeing a rewritten version with more of the detail and cross-referencing that's been laid out in discussion. (For what it's worth, I did process the "the Foundation will do anything" implication from the article alone, and it was pretty effective- just not my favorite thing about the piece.)

I'll stick with a neutral vote and see what happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could benefit from a heimdall tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They are there to ensure that, if any species other than humanity is occupying a target planet, they will cease to exist. The causality disruptors will make it as though non-human intelligences never existed, as though humanity evolved naturally on their new home, your home. The Ethics Committee doesn't know this.

And… downvoted.

Using reality benders of this power and magnitude to remove and replace all sentient life on another planet is either specific to the point of ridiculousness (if that's all they can do) or a gross misuse of their possible application (if they can do other things). Also like many other people pointed out, aren't there enough lifeless rocks in space to colonize? This seems edgy just for the purpose of being edgy.

Also, nothing goes past the Ethics Committee. Nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only complaint: I was confused by docs alfa and beta, the cross-reference more than they should. What if you unify them?

About your skip:
- An interesting headcanon of how the modern Foundation "started".
- A horrible horrible failsafe for those People-How-Dealt-With-Dangerous-Toys. Very SCPF. I like it.
- I think it needs more. Specifically, scientists from the 1900's trying to put into words unfathomable technology and leeching the hell out of it.

+1, keep going!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because this wasn't as popular as a 001 Proposal. -10 is a bad place to be, so I self-deleted and moved her to the mainlist. I might try again sometime, but for now, it stays where it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohhh that's an interesting theory…Why isn't this one a 001 proposal?

item.title: Re: Regarding Tags
item.content:encoded: Made some edits. Navigation should be a bit easier now.

item.title: Re: Regarding Tags
item.content:encoded: The parent page uses the listPages module (same code as SCP-1173. The secondary pages link to each other so that the order people read the pieces in is random, but most people are likely to read both. If I parent the two pages to this one, this page will link to itself. If you have a good alternative, I might implement it.

item.title: Re: Regarding Tags
item.content:encoded: I got dizzy trying to navigate through those pages, do me a big favor and throw up some links to the parent page please. I will revisit tagging them later. But as it is right now, it's too convoluted for me.

item.title: Regarding Tags
item.content:encoded:
I noticed that someone tagged this not too long ago, and I appreciate that. However, I'm not sure the tagging is complete. Specifically, the tags applied only seem to apply to one version of the document. The other version requires another set of tags entirely. If someone can provide input on this, I'd appreciate it.

Dropping this link for the sake of my own future reference.

item.title: Re: Gonna rewrite this motherf*cker big time
item.content:encoded: I have to admire you for your ambition…

item.title: Re: Gonna rewrite this motherfucker big time
item.content:encoded:
Here's the deal, though. I've seen three kinds of reactions to new authors: Downvote to oblivion, upvote to Cloud 9, and "meh." All of my work so far has been "meh." I want my work to be well-received because it means I did my job as an author at entertaining people. This SCP has garnered a lot of neutral votes and a fairly large number of downvotes. The proposed edits were specifically in response to complaints, not just me coming up with random ideas. And, as I said, I don't feel I gave this idea the sort of treatment it deserves.

I was planning on sandboxing a rewrite, though, so thank you for confirming that idea to be sound.

item.title: Re: Gonna rewrite this motherfucker big time
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't call a total rating of +25 (with 9 downvotes) "floundering"… Maybe not what you hoped for, but it's not like it's in danger of deletion for the forseeable future.

So I'd say leave it alone for now. At best, this SCP does better. At worst, your edits cause the rating to pull a Titanic. Might be best if you prep a rewrite just in case this falls to deletion threshold. Plus it gives you more experience writing SCPs.

item.title: Gonna rewrite this motherfucker big time
item.content:encoded:
So, this was too overambitious for an author of my skill and for the amount of effort I put in, it would be unfair to say otherwise. I basically just took my ideas, drafted them, showed it to people so I could say it wasn't a coldpost, then posted. The story is more complicated than that, but I just made an accurate summary.

Because of this, this SCP is floundering. The idea deserves more than I gave it. I'll be making edits in order to attempt to reverse neutral votes, and will eventually do a full rewrite, abandoning the structure already presented entirely. Edits will potentially include:

  • Removing some of the debatable geopolitical details (specifically, the Holy Emperor's Council is probably giving away, and the Tsar's Seers are probably likewise going away)
  • Adding a French GOI (makes a wee bit more sense than Japanese and Russian ones1)
  • Changing the year of ratification of Document B from 1915 to 1895, 1905, or 1925.
  • Removing the references to holograms; nobody understood that's what I was getting at anyway.
  • Possibly implying that one ship exists solely to fix Earth from afar.
  • Removing the blackboxes for the sake of crosslinking to SCP-1795.
  • Crosslinking to SCP-2003 or making the (admittedly almost nonexistent) connection to 2003 more obvious. Alternatively, making another reason for the ships to turn on. Will consult Kalinin very hard on that one, because it's his skip.
  • Adding technobabble, logistical details, and further emphasizing Foundation-sponsored genocide and other wee things to make the picture I'm painting more detailed.
  • Addressing wee things pointed out in Kalinin's line-by-line.
  • Adding actual supplements for the sake of making the rest less of a pain in the ass.

Opinions on this? Suggestions? Advice on whether to delete in order to clean the slate? All opinions are appreciated.

Footnotes
1. I'm not saying I'm discarding the eastern GOIs from my headcanon, because they'll still be there; they'll just be less buddy-buddy with SCPF and precursors, and won't be involved in this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So let me get this straight. SCP-2237 is an super fail safe like SCP-2000 if a K class event occurs. Unlike SCP-2000 this is supposed to be the supreme fail safe, you leave the planet permanently. While I try to appreciate what you are trying to do, it was distinctly underwhelming.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still doesn't make sense if they have all that crap like weaponized reality benders.

Broseph, the problem with this article is that it's a starship what colonized other planets cuz we done fucked up here, then just expect folk to assume more shit done fucked up to bring us here in the wrong way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well-written and all, but the similarities to 2000 are too big to ignore. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They didn't turn back the clock. They were living with modern1 technology for awhile, but something happened to knock them back to square one. I mean, 100,000 years is a long time. A lot can happen.

Footnotes
1. For them, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your head cannon is irrelevant. Enjoyment of the article shouldn't be dependent on knowing all this that and the other that gets you out of explaining x arbitrary restrictions or limitations on the starship colonizing other planets. Also why turn the clock back when showing up at a new planet? Can't they just use said reality bender to make everybody think it's still the 24'th century and nothing happened? Why go backwards for no reason?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The disruptors can only do so much. For example, they can make fake archaeological evidence of human evolution and change an atmosphere that's similar to Earth's to be identical to or better than Earth's, and they can undo something as obvious as a technological civilization, but they can't undo an entire planet being covered in self-spreading ink or Keter Cakes or something like that. Plus, as I already said, some of the ships are meant to go to planets built by SCP-1795; those don't need the disruptors in order to become suitable for human life. That's why they don't just go to Venus, Mars, or some dead planet orbiting Sirius; they have to go somewhere that's pretty much already suitable for human life.

Don't worry about off-the-wall shit I say because I have an extensive headcanon on this that isn't immediately obvious on the first readthrough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what you were attempting here, but there's just not any impact for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite liked this as a skip, but it was underwhelming as an 001. To specifically address the problems anyone might have with that, it's not a matter of expecting different qualities for 001 proposals, but rather a certain type of article. Just as a -j or tales are different than a mainlister (and what works for one might not work for others), a 001 is different from them all as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok. If this drops below -5, I'll delete it. In the meantime, I'll work on this in my sandbox between other projects.

Quite frankly, if I can write something that's within neural-vote territory for someone with standards as high as your own, I'll consider the work a success. I appreciate your input and hope to someday write something that you might be able to enjoy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm always willing to reevaluate after an extensive rewrite. However, it's going to take a major reworking to address the points above, and even then you've got a challenge ahead of you if your aim is to make "how far the Foundation is willing to go" the main theme of the piece, for numerous reasons. You'll want to weigh the issues of one particular user against those structural obstacles.

The geopolitics aren't my main issue really. In any event, I just don't see how it would work; moving the date five years isn't going to make a difference, especially in the case of relations between Russia and Japan (being closer to the Russo-Japanese War isn't going to improve matters any). And that doesn't speak to the main problems, which are A) four nations that each view themselves as Great Powers in the traditional 19th century sense are unlikely to cooperate that closely, and B) each of the four has vastly differing cultural norms, meaning their views of consensus reality are likely to be vastly different, assuming of course that all of them want to isolate such things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait whoa there, didn't get distributors installed on their ships? Why is there a shortage of them all of the sudden? Also that still doesn't make sense, if we can just wipe everything away, why don't we and just fix everything? What is stopping us. Also why the turning civilization backwards after colonization? Why can't we just find a place where everything isn't crazy broken? Why do we gota wipe out a planet to colonize it? Is there suddenly a shortage of dead rocks out there?

Just why this? Why that? Really. From what you are spelling out here, it seems like were doings things because the plot demands them, not because they make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I was a little nonplussed by the whole thing, but after going from Alfa to Bravo, I caught the thread linking the two together and upvoted for that. Actually, the most confusing thing about this was which page to upvote. I started out on Bravo, but after clicking back a few times, I'm pretty sure I ended up on Alfa. Wikidot is weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, man. Writing is hard.

Do you suppose you would ever reread this following a rewrite tailored to address these specific complaints?

Also, while it's on my mind, would moving the date back five years and elaborating on the nature of the two Eastern organizations, making them less proto-Foundation-ish, improve your opinion of the geopolitical aspects of Document B?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Before I get into the article itself, I have to point out some philosophical issues. I don't really care for the line of reasoning espoused by several commenters, in this thread and the previous one when this was an 001, that this is somehow not good enough as a Proposal but good enough for the mainlist.

Just as I think it's erroneous when people say that a piece isn't good enough to be a mainlister, but it's good enough as a -J or a tale, I think the subtext that somehow the bar of quality is different for a mainlist article and a 001 Proposal is in error. For all of this piece's flaws, it was written as a Proposal, a format that has very specific criteria that set it apart from a mainlist entry. The fact that it was ported over into the mainlist format with no changes suggests that the operating assumption is that the bar for "good enough" on the mainlist is lower than that for proposals. That is a stance that I reject wholeheartedly. The two formats are different. It's like saying that a novella is inherently better than a short story.

As to the article itself:

+ line-by-line
- hide text

This document has been ratified by the leading members of the American Secure Containment Initiative, Her Majesty's Foundation for the Secure Containment of the Paranormal, the Holy Emperor's Council on Unearthly Matters, and the Tsar's Seers on July 9, 1915, as one of the first documents of the SCP Foundation, a new organization formed from the aforementioned organizations in order to better perform the functions of Securing that which cannot yet be understood, Containing that which would threaten the public's perception of normality, and Protecting the public from threats preternatural or otherwise not yet understood.

As I said in the chat, I don't care for the fact that disparate powers like the US, UK, Japan and Russia all happen to have Foundation analogue proto-organizations. Even between the two closest allies in that listing (the US and the UK), there were very different worldviews, cultures, and national aims that would have informed how these governments would have gone about dealing with the anomalous. The notion that all four powers just happen to share the idea that this stuff should be squirreled away and kept from public view, especially at a time when they're busy mass-murdering the Central Powers and putting down revolutionaries, flies in the face of any notion of realism on the part of your geopolitical setting.

Euclid (Moderate Containment Difficulty)

The reminder text seems kind of goofy. There's three (or four) classifications, and even in the early days of the Foundation I'm not sure that's necessary.

In the event of a hostile takeover

I could be mistaken, but I think "hostile takeover" is more of a contemporary business term rather than early 20th century military parlance.

and incorporeal automata capable of speech

"incorporeal"? I have no idea what this is referring to.

■Bachelor's degrees in one or more of the following fields

Assuming that what you're meaning to convey is that only the best of the best are to be selected for duty here, a bachelor's doesn't make a whole lot of sense. That's basically entry-level qualifications for starting work in a related field, not a high bar for only the most experienced and knowledgable to clear.

As per the Trinity Protocol, the existence of SCP-2237 is not classified; however, all civilians and individuals lacking sufficient security clearance who ascertain the location of an SCP-2237 instance are to be administered Class-C amnestics.

…what? So it's not classified, but anyone that finds out about it gets mind-wiped? That makes no sense at all.

Conceptually, as I and others have stated previously, this is at heart a reset button. That, of course, invites the comparison to SCP-2000. Now, in and of itself that isn't a problem, though it does mean that one would have to work that much harder to make a retread of such a well-known concept interesting.

This piece, though, really suffers in comparison to that one. Where SCP-2000 sold the reader on the technical capabilities required to pull off such a massive undertaking as rebuilding society after a catastrophic event by balancing realistic detail and brevity, this article just kind of handwaves away the logistical issues. By assuming that we'll have FTL drives, stasis technology, terraforming capabilities that can be localized on a single ship and a rolodex of Earth-like planets in the future, this article loses any real connection to the present-day reader and just becomes a showcase of standard issue sci-fi technology.

The exception of course is the genocidal reality benders. This is apparently the part where this article is supposed to differentiate itself from SCP-2000, but if that's the case, this aspect is woefully underdeveloped. There's no explanation as to what this "wipe everything from existence" mechanism really is aside from a couple of sentences in a memo. There's no acknowledgement anywhere of the scale of what the Foundation is doing in the documentation aside from the aforementioned couple of sentences. And there's no description or viewpoint of any sort from the populations being booted for the sake of humanity, so there's no real horror or pathos because there's nothing to identify with. You've brought out the concept of complete, repeated genocide, but it's not developed even a little. This makes that aspect of the article come across as thin, as well as cheaply treated.

Finally, the article closes with whoever is accessing the file (presumably Bright) ordering a colonization. With absolutely no context or clue as to what is necessitating that. This coda reinforces the overall feeling that this is a collection of undeveloped ideas rather than any sort of cohesive narrative.

Ambition alone does not equate to a successful article, either as a proposal or a mainlist entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. That is an interesting theory. Hadn't thought of that. There's a couple SCPs what were outright created by bixbies; it makes sense that Foundation-loving bixbies might accidentally do the same. Yeah, your theory makes sense, and no, the Foundation wouldn't know about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since you ask…

The obvious implication is that humanity originated on another planet, which it seeded - possibly amongst others - after a K-class event.

But the more subtle implication - at least in my headcanon, with 2237 being a displaced 001 candidate - is that reality bending devices don't completely erase the previous reality, and don't create a pristine one in its place. The "terraforming" (terror-forming? Heh.) process would create…anomalies.

And the more the foundation uses the device to set up shop after escaping the keter anomalies, the more get created.

So, would they know this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmm'yep. We done did displace the natives, but we failed to properly wipe them out because of their anatomical similarity to us. We started out small, but eventually, with a little help from Eldritch Gods pretending to be regular Gods1, we became top dog. Soon enough, they'll be asking their due, and we'll be paying for our crimes in kind.

Life kinda sucks for everyone in the Foundationverse.

Footnotes
1. As if there's a difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. But I'm unclear on something.

I believe I have it right that the SCP overlays 'Humanity' on a designated planet in case of catastrophe of the homeworld.

Does that mean Humanity is not indigenous to Earth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I'll do more Pattern Screamer stuff when I write the talking dog and the transuniversal biopunk cyborgs.

I'm dead serious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1 because no Pattern Screamers. Okay not really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't wanna overuse the word "modern;" the Alfa bit was not written in 1915; HMFSCP is an established thing, go ask Djoric; Quantum Engineering isn't even a thing, the document is from THE FUTURE!!(I'm making fun of myself, don't worry about it); I think the rest is just personal thoughts and doesn't need to be addressed by me. Anything else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Scattered minor thoughts
- Scattered minor thoughts

technology not yet available to baseline humanity

"Baseline" in 1915? Anachronism breaks flow.

Document 2237 Alfa.

Given the historical and cultural setting, the spelling would almost certainly be "Alpha".

Her Majesty's Foundation for the Secure Containment of the Paranormal

Torchwood!

Bachelor's degree…Quantum Engineering

I'm fairly sure that's impossible. Quantum stuff is post-PhD.

if any species other than humanity is occupying a target planet, they will cease to exist

That's what I love about the foundation's documents - the horror is buried in asides.

The Ethics Committee doesn't know this

So the ethics committee okayed what the O5s told them was a teraforming project. Office politics on an intergalactic scale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation already has measures on Earth for that, namely other Thaumiels. If those can't fix it, it's probably best to run away. If you look at the dates, these things were built to deal with one specific problem.

Plus, somebody has been building planets just for us. That's what's behind the blackboxes in :a. The O5s who didn't get the causality disruptors installed on their ships had their ships programmed to go there.

Really, though, it just made sense to have an interstellar ark as a Thaumiel SCP; it's a very Foundation thing to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it's that, then that raises even more problems. Problems such as if you are going somewhere else to start anew with the ability to start fresh, why in the hell wouldn't you cleanse the world you landed on of anomalous nonsense just to start out with?
